source.subject: Console: surfacing incident type/owner for Ops + CS (permissioning + rollout comms)
source.body:
From: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dylan Brooks <dylan_brooks@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel_kim@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwoodinference.com>, Aisha Bello <aisha_bello@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Jan 20, 2026 at 9:14 AM PT
Subject: Console: surfacing incident type/owner for Ops + CS (permissioning + rollout comms)

Looping folks in on PM-913. We’re at the point where the Console can show incident_type/subtype + resolved owner (from incident-bot/control plane) for internal ops workflows.

Open questions we need to lock this week:

1) What exactly is visible to CS?
- CS has asked for visibility so they can answer “what’s going on?” quickly in escalations.
- SRE/Eng are (rightfully) concerned about CS seeing internal-only fields that can be misinterpreted or shared verbatim.

2) Edit permissions:
- My preference: CS can view but cannot modify incident classification/owner in Console.
- All edits still go through incident-bot + on-call process.

3) Rollout comms + timing:
- We need a clean message for CS: “what you’ll see, what you shouldn’t promise, and where to look for updates.”

Attachments:
- console-ops-incidents-wireframe-v3.png (draft)
- rbac-matrix-incident-fields-cs-ops.xlsx (initial pass)

Proposal (strawman):
- Ops view (SRE/Eng): show incident_type, subtype, owner, severity, tier, SLA timers, contributing systems, internal notes links.
- CS view: show incident_type (top-level), severity, tier, current status, customer-safe summary template, and owner team name (no direct paging/escalation info).

Can we confirm:
- Whether “subtype” should be CS-visible?
- Whether “owner team” should be CS-visible (I think yes, but read-only)?

If we can align by EOD Thu, we can make it into next week’s Console deployment.

Marissa
Product, Redwood Console


---

From: Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan_brooks@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel_kim@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwoodinference.com>, Aisha Bello <aisha_bello@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Jan 20, 2026 at 10:02 AM PT
Subject: Re: Console: surfacing incident type/owner for Ops + CS (permissioning + rollout comms)

+1 to CS having read-only access.

From CS’s perspective, the most useful fields are:
- incident_type (top-level)
- impacted tier (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)
- customer impact summary (even if it’s auto-generated / canned)
- current mitigation status (mitigating/monitoring/resolved)

Re: subtype—cautious. Subtypes can be too “inside baseball” and can read like root cause.
Example: “Routing/Fallback Misbehavior: policy mismatch” becomes something a customer might latch onto even if we aren’t sure.

Owner team name: yes, but I’d avoid showing escalation channels / on-call rotations to CS. We can keep routing to SRE in the comms loop.

Also: we should add a small tooltip in CS view: “Incident type is operational classification and may be updated as investigation progresses.”

Dev
Support Escalation Mapping Lead


---

From: Dylan Brooks <dylan_brooks@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel_kim@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwoodinference.com>, Aisha Bello <aisha_bello@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Jan 20, 2026 at 11:18 AM PT
Subject: Re: Console: surfacing incident type/owner for Ops + CS (permissioning + rollout comms)

Agree with the direction.

Implementation detail:
- The API (PR-28503) already returns incident_type + subtype + resolved_owner.
- Console can choose which fields to render based on RBAC.

RBAC suggestion:
- Create a Console permission: incident_taxonomy.read (CS gets)
- Keep incident_taxonomy.write restricted to SRE + incident commanders (and only through incident-bot for v1)

One thing to watch:
- If CS can see the resolved_owner, they will naturally DM that team directly. We should explicitly tell CS to continue using #incidents + the designated CS liaison workflow for Sev0/Sev1.

If we want to enforce that: we can show “Owner: <team> (internal)” with a tooltip: “For updates, use incident channel; do not page directly.”

Dylan
Tooling Integration Lead


---

From: Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan_brooks@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel_kim@redwoodinference.com>, Aisha Bello <aisha_bello@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Jan 20, 2026 at 12:07 PM PT
Subject: Re: Console: surfacing incident type/owner for Ops + CS (permissioning + rollout comms)

+1 on keeping edits out of Console for now.

From the control plane/owner mapping side:
- “resolved_owner” is derived from the versioned mapping config.
- We will have cases where incident_type is “Unknown/Unclassified” early; owner will fall back to SRE.

I’m worried about CS seeing subtype because subtype is exactly where we’ll iterate v1.1 and we don’t want CS docs to break every time a label changes.

Recommendation:
- CS view shows only top-level incident_type and a “category description” string that we control (customer-safe language).
- Ops view shows incident_type + subtype + fallback_reason + mapping version (for audit/debug).

Ethan
Platform Control Plane


---

From: Aisha Bello <aisha_bello@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan_brooks@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel_kim@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Jan 20, 2026 at 1:26 PM PT
Subject: Re: Console: surfacing incident type/owner for Ops + CS (permissioning + rollout comms)

Adding one more ask from SRE reporting side:
- In Ops view we should show “incident_type last updated at” + “updated by” so we can audit churn.
- For CS view, I’m fine with hiding the raw subtype, but we should show when the type was last updated (so CS can tell if it’s stale).

Also, please don’t expose remediation SLA timers to CS initially. CS will read “time-to-fix” as a promise. We can provide SLA status (“On track / At risk / Breached”) later once the policy + comms are rolled out.

Aisha
SLO/SLA Dashboards Owner


---

From: Rachel Kim <rachel_kim@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan_brooks@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwoodinference.com>, Aisha Bello <aisha_bello@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Jan 20, 2026 at 3:03 PM PT
Subject: Re: Console: surfacing incident type/owner for Ops + CS (permissioning + rollout comms)

From the enterprise comms perspective:

What’s safe/valuable for CS:
- Top-level incident type (customer-safe label)
- Impact scope (regions/tier), high-level symptoms (e.g., “elevated 5xx” / “increased latency”) without asserting root cause
- Owner team name (fine, but with clear guidance not to DM/page)

What to avoid:
- Subtype and internal contributing systems
- “Fallback reason” enums
- SLA timers or due-times

Rollout comms:
- I suggest we send a short internal CS note + update the escalation playbook page with screenshots.
- We should include a line that incident type can change within the first ~30 minutes as investigation progresses.

Rachel
Enterprise Comms / SLA Stakeholder


---

From: Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan_brooks@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel_kim@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwoodinference.com>, Aisha Bello <aisha_bello@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 21, 2026 at 4:27 PM PT
Subject: Re: Console: surfacing incident type/owner for Ops + CS (permissioning + rollout comms)

Decision:
- CS gets read-only access to top-level incident type (no subtype), tier, severity, status, and a customer-safe description.
- CS does not get SLA timers/due-times in v1.
- Owner team name is visible, with explicit guidance not to page/DM owners directly; CS routes through incident channel + CS liaison.

Marissa/Dylan: please align on copy + tooltips and ship behind the CS role flag first. Aisha: +1 to include “last updated at” in both views.

Let’s aim for:
- Console change shipped next week (behind role flag)
- CS comms + playbook update same day

Sean
Exec Sponsor (SRE)

Date: Wed, Jan 21, 2026 at 4:27 PM PT
Subject: Re: Console: surfacing incident type/owner for Ops + CS (permissioning + rollout comms)

Decision:
- CS gets read-only access to top-level incident type (no subtype), tier, severity, status, and a customer-safe description.
- CS does not get SLA timers/due-times in v1.
- Owner team name is visible, with explicit guidance not to page/DM owners directly; CS routes through incident channel + CS liaison.

Marissa/Dylan: please align on copy + tooltips and ship behind the CS role flag first. Aisha: +1 to include “last updated at” in both views.

Let’s aim for:
- Console change shipped next week (behind role flag)
- CS comms + playbook update same day

Sean
Exec Sponsor (SRE)

source.subject: Re: ADR review: Shadow traffic routing architecture (perf + fail-open)
source.body:
From: Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwoodinference.com>, Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Ravi Menon <ravi_menon@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan_wright@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia_benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sun, Jan 18, 2026 at 9:12 AM PT
Subject: ADR review: Shadow traffic routing architecture (perf + fail-open)
Attachments: adr-shadow-traffic-routing-architecture-v3.pdf, adr-shadow-metrics-data-model-and-cardinality-v2.pdf

Team — I read through the updated ADRs (routing architecture + metrics/data model). Directionally aligned with “inline mirroring w/ hard guardrails” given the rollout goal, but I need a few clarifications before I sign off.

Key concerns/questions:

1) Performance impact on the user path
- The ADR states “shadowing must be invisible to the control response.” Please spell out *exactly* where the shadow call is executed and what guarantees we’re making that the control response is not waiting on any shadow work (including serialization, hashing, or enqueue).
- What’s the measured overhead in the worst case (p99) when shadowing is enabled at 1% and at 5%? Even if the shadow target is down/slow.
- I want explicit budget numbers in the ADR (e.g., <= X ms added at p50/p99) and where we enforce them.

2) Fail-open semantics (must be unambiguous)
- Define “fail-open” in operational terms. I want: (a) no added tail latency on control path beyond budget, (b) control success unaffected by shadow target errors, (c) shadow target/scoring failures do not trigger retries that pressure the control plane/data plane.
- If the shadow path times out, what’s emitted (events, counters) and what is dropped?

3) Rollout safety + kill switch
- What’s the rollout plan for enabling this in prod (flags, staged tenants, % ramp)?
- Where is the circuit breaker anchored (per-route? per-tenant? global)?
- I need a clear “big red button” that SRE can use if we see metrics ingestion blowups or unexpected latency impact.

4) Metrics cardinality / data pipeline blast radius
- The metrics ADR is generally good, but I want confirmation we are not emitting per-request high-cardinality labels (prompt_id, user_id, raw route name, etc.).
- Please add an explicit “do not include” list to the schema section and the enforcement mechanism.

Can you reply with (a) answers inline and (b) the minimal edits you’ll make to ADR text so the decisions and consequences are locked?

If we can close this today, I’m comfortable with moving to Phase 1.

— Priya
Priya Natarajan
Platform Exec Sponsor
Redwood Inference


---

From: Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwoodinference.com>, Ravi Menon <ravi_menon@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan_wright@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia_benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sun, Jan 18, 2026 at 10:03 AM PT
Subject: Re: ADR review: Shadow traffic routing architecture (perf + fail-open)

Thanks Priya — very fair asks. Mina and I will reply with specifics.

Quick initial answers (more below):
- Control response never awaits shadow request completion. Shadow call is dispatched after control request is handed off to runtime and we have the control stream handle.
- We’re proposing a strict timeout (e.g., 150ms) on the shadow dispatch path *and* hard CPU budget for serialization.
- Circuit breaker will be per-route and per-tenant with a global override flag.

We’ll push ADR edits today and point you to the diffs.

Ethan


---

From: Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwoodinference.com>
To: Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwoodinference.com>, Ravi Menon <ravi_menon@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan_wright@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia_benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sun, Jan 18, 2026 at 11:21 AM PT
Subject: Re: ADR review: Shadow traffic routing architecture (perf + fail-open)

Replying inline below.

> 1) Performance impact on the user path

Where the shadow call is executed:
- Data plane / routing layer: after deterministic bucketing decides “shadow: yes/no”, we enqueue an async “shadow dispatch task” onto a bounded, low-priority executor.
- The control response lifecycle (HTTP streaming) proceeds on the primary request executor. We do *not* await any future/promise from the shadow dispatch.

Guarantees:
- Shadow dispatch is best-effort. If the executor queue is full, we drop the shadow dispatch immediately (and increment a drop counter) rather than blocking.
- Any serialization for telemetry uses a capped payload size and is performed on the shadow executor. Control path only computes the request fingerprint (fast hash) + increments counters.

Overhead numbers:
- We don’t have prod p99 yet; we have early bench results from perf-canary on a representative chat workload.
  - 1% sampling: +0.1ms p50 / +0.6ms p99 on control path.
  - 5% sampling: +0.3ms p50 / +1.8ms p99 on control path.
- Worst case (shadow target down): overhead is essentially the same because we fail at dispatch (timeout/circuit breaker) and never block the control request.

Edits we’ll make to ADR:
- Add explicit control-path latency budget: “Shadowing must add <=2ms at p99 on control path at 5% sampling under shadow-target failure.”
- Add explicit statement: “Control request completion is independent of shadow dispatch completion.”

> 2) Fail-open semantics

Operational definition we’re putting in ADR:
(a) Control path latency budget enforced via bounded executor + non-blocking dispatch + timeout.
(b) Shadow target errors do not affect control status code/body.
(c) No retries from data plane. Retries (if any) happen only in the async scoring pipeline, not in the request router.

What’s emitted/dropped on shadow timeout:
- Emitted: a single “shadow_dispatch_outcome=timeout” counter, with safe dimensions (tenant, route_id bucketed, shadow_variant_id).
- Dropped: we do not emit the full request/response pair event because we don’t have a shadow response.

> 3) Rollout safety + kill switch

Rollout plan:
- Feature flags at three layers:
  1) router.shadow_dispatch_enabled (global)
  2) tenant.shadow_enabled (per-tenant allowlist)
  3) route.shadow_policy_enabled (per-route)
- Ramp: internal tenant -> 1 Dedicated beta tenant -> expand beta -> GA.

Circuit breaker anchoring:
- Per-route, per-tenant breaker based on: shadow dispatch queue saturation, shadow target error rate/timeouts, and shadow telemetry ingestion lag signals.
- Global override flag for oncall.

Kill switch:
- Global flag + SRE runbook action will disable shadow dispatch immediately (no deploy). This will also stop writing shadow-pair events.

> 4) Metrics cardinality

Yes: no per-request IDs, no prompt_id, no raw customer_id.
- We use hashed/rotated tenant_id internally but not exposed as a label in metrics; dashboards use tenant scoping via access control not via labels.
- route_id is a stable internal identifier and we’ll bucket if necessary for shared dashboards.

We’ll add “do-not-include list” to ADR (prompt_id, user_id, request_id, raw route name, arbitrary headers, tool names) and validation in schema layer.

Mina

We agree on hard constraints and enforcement:
- Event schema will reject/downgrade any unapproved dimensions at ingestion time (allowlist-only). Any non-allowlisted tags are dropped and recorded in an internal “dropped_dimensions_count” metric (no labels).
- We’re also rate-limiting per-tenant shadow events independent of request QPS (this protects the analytics pipeline if a customer misconfigures sampling).

What we’ll add to the ADR text:
- A dedicated “cardinality guardrails” section with:

Also: for dashboards, we’ll prefer rollup tables keyed by (tenant, experiment_id, variant_id, metric_name, time_window) rather than raw event queries.

Logan

Subject: ADR review: Shadow traffic routing architecture (perf + fail-open)
Attachments: adr-shadow-traffic-routing-architecture-v3.pdf, adr-shadow-metrics-data-model-and-cardinality-v2.pdf

Team — I read through the updated ADRs (routing architecture + metrics/data model). Directionally aligned with “inline mirroring w/ hard guardrails” given the rollout goal, but I need a few clarifications before I sign off.

Key concerns/questions:

1) Performance impact on the user path
- The ADR states “shadowing must be invisible to the control response.” Please spell out *exactly* where the shadow call is executed and what guarantees we’re making that the control response is not waiting on any shadow work (including serialization, hashing, or enqueue).
- What’s the measured overhead in the worst case (p99) when shadowing is enabled at 1% and at 5%? Even if the shadow target is down/slow.
- I want explicit budget numbers in the ADR (e.g., <= X ms added at p50/p99) and where we enforce them.

2) Fail-open semantics (must be unambiguous)
- Define “fail-open” in operational terms. I want: (a) no added tail latency on control path beyond budget, (b) control success unaffected by shadow target errors, (c) shadow target/scoring failures do not trigger retries that pressure the control plane/data plane.
- If the shadow path times out, what’s emitted (events, counters) and what is dropped?

3) Rollout safety + kill switch
- What’s the rollout plan for enabling this in prod (flags, staged tenants, % ramp)?
- Where is the circuit breaker anchored (per-route? per-tenant? global)?
- I need a clear “big red button” that SRE can use if we see metrics ingestion blowups or unexpected latency impact.

4) Metrics cardinality / data pipeline blast radius
- The metrics ADR is generally good, but I want confirmation we are not emitting per-request high-cardinality labels (prompt_id, user_id, raw route name, etc.).
- Please add an explicit “do not include” list to the schema section and the enforcement mechanism.

Can you reply with (a) answers inline and (b) the minimal edits you’ll make to ADR text so the decisions and consequences are locked?

If we can close this today, I’m comfortable with moving to Phase 1.

— Priya
Priya Natarajan
Platform Exec Sponsor
Redwood Inference

Cc: Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwoodinference.com>, Ravi Menon <ravi_menon@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan_wright@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia_benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sun, Jan 18, 2026 at 2:54 PM PT
Subject: Re: ADR review: Shadow traffic routing architecture (perf + fail-open)

SRE addendum — what we need for oncall comfort.

+1 to explicitly stating the budget in the ADR.

Two additional requirements I’d like captured:
1) Shadow dispatch must have a hard cap on concurrent in-flight shadow requests per host (to avoid noisy-neighbor CPU and connection pool issues).
2) Circuit breaker thresholds + paging policy must be specified (even if “initial defaults”) so we don’t end up debating during an incident.

Proposed initial breaker thresholds (can tune in beta):
- If shadow dispatch queue is >80% full for 2m -> start dropping shadow (breaker open)
- If shadow dispatch timeout rate >5% for 5m (per route+tenant) -> breaker open
- If shadow telemetry ingestion lag >10m -> breaker open globally (to protect downstream)

Kill switch: please ensure we can disable via config/flag and it propagates in <5 minutes.

If you incorporate those, I’m good.

Sean Gallagher
SRE Lead
Redwood Inference


---

From: Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwoodinference.com>, Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Ravi Menon <ravi_menon@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan_wright@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia_benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sun, Jan 18, 2026 at 4:48 PM PT
Subject: Re: ADR review: Shadow traffic routing architecture (perf + fail-open)

This addresses the core concerns.

Approval with conditions:
- Add the explicit control-path latency budget and enforcement mechanism to the routing ADR.
- Add Sean’s two SRE requirements (in-flight cap + breaker thresholds + propagation time for the kill switch).
- Add the “do-not-include” list + allowlist enforcement language to the metrics ADR.

Once those edits are in (and linked in the ADR history), consider the architecture approved for Phase 1.

Please also make sure the rollout plan references the flag names and ownership (who can flip global vs per-tenant vs per-route).

— Priya

Priya Natarajan
Platform Exec Sponsor
Redwood Inference

source.subject: SDK parity kickoff + exec visibility (8-week plan)
source.body:
From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>
To: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 15, 2026 at 9:12 AM PST
Subject: SDK parity kickoff + exec visibility (8-week plan)
Attachments: sdk-parity-kickoff-onepager-v1.pdf; sdk-parity-timeline-and-risks-2026-01-15.xlsx

Nikhil, Ethan —

Kicking off the cross-language SDK parity initiative (Python/TS/Go) focused on auth + retries/backoff + timeouts + streaming semantics.

Why now: Support + sales are both feeling the “works in TS but not in Python/Go” pain, especially around 429s, timeouts, and stream disconnects (recent examples: SUP-29438, SUP-29477, SUP-29505). We also had an internal incident suspicion of client retry storms during elevated 429s; we need consistent defaults and guardrails.

Scope (in):
- Auth resolution order (explicit param vs env vars), safe secret handling + redaction
- Retry policy: retryable codes/network errors, exponential backoff w/ jitter, respect Retry-After
- Timeout model (connect/read/overall mapping per language)
- Streaming semantics: disconnect handling, partial output surfaces, conservative retry guidance w/ idempotency
- Error taxonomy / typed errors with request_id propagation
- Conformance test suite + fixtures in CI across all 3 SDKs
- Docs + quickstarts parity + migration notes

Non-goals (out):
- Major API redesign or new auth schemes
- Deep model semantics changes

Timeline: 8 weeks (audit -> ADR approvals -> implementation -> beta rollout -> stable)
- Week 1–2: audit + parity matrix, reproduce top incidents
- Week 2–3: publish standards/ADRs w/ Security + SRE signoff
- Week 3–6: implementation + conformance tests + docs
- Week 6–8: beta + monitoring + rollout / migration guide

Key risks we’re explicitly managing:
1) Breaking changes / behavior drift: e.g., default timeouts and retryable error sets differ today. We’ll minimize breaking changes; where unavoidable, gate behind opt-in flags and ship deprecation warnings first.
2) Retry storms / platform load: new retry defaults can amplify load during partial outages. We’ll cap max attempts, honor Retry-After, and instrument retry_count + backoff_ms. We’ll ship dashboards + rollback triggers before broad rollout.
3) Streaming retries: we will be conservative. If we retry blindly, we risk duplicates. Guidance: do not auto-retry mid-stream; if customer retries, recommend idempotency keys + request_id correlation.

Asks:
- Nikhil: OK to treat this as an exec-visible reliability/customer friction initiative (small but real breaking-change risk), with weekly check-in until stable release?
- Ethan: We’ll need platform support for gateway consistency signals (Retry-After on 429, consistent request_id + error fields). Can you confirm priority + timing for those dependencies so SDK changes don’t ship “half supported”?

I’m happy to walk through the one-pager live; main goal is alignment on scope and how aggressive we can be on defaults vs flags.

— Monica
Monica Patel
Director, SDK Engineering | Redwood Inference


From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 15, 2026 at 10:03 AM PST
Subject: Re: SDK parity kickoff + exec visibility (8-week plan)

Thanks Monica — makes sense.

Platform deps I see (some already in flight):
- Ensure Retry-After is always set on 429 responses (seconds + date formats) and preserved through proxying
- Ensure request_id is consistently present for streaming + non-streaming errors
- Harmonize error code enum so SDKs can classify retryable vs terminal without guessing on message text

I can take point on the gateway pieces; I’ll tie them to ENG-5130 and give you a concrete date by EOD. Biggest question: are you expecting the SDKs to be strict about Retry-After parsing from day 1, or do you want to keep fallback behavior if it’s missing?

Also: please share the draft error taxonomy fields once you have them (request_id, status_code, code, retryable, etc.). That will help us ensure the gateway response shape lines up.

— Ethan
Ethan Park
Platform Engineering | Redwood Inference


From: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 15, 2026 at 10:28 AM PST
Subject: Re: SDK parity kickoff + exec visibility (8-week plan)

Yes — let’s treat this as exec-visible.

A couple points I want nailed down up front:
1) Defaults vs opt-in flags: I’m OK with flags for behavior that can change customer unit economics or perceived reliability (timeouts/retries). But I don’t want a long tail of “old vs new” modes. Please propose a deprecation timeline (when we flip defaults).
2) Rollout monitoring/rollback: before we broaden rollout, I want clear rollback triggers (e.g., retry rate spike, 429 amplification, timeout rate, stream disconnects). Also need clarity on where these metrics are surfaced.
3) Customer messaging: coordinate w/ PM/docs so we don’t surprise enterprise customers. Breaking changes need migration notes and a recommended config block for each language.

Ethan: agree on gateway consistency. We should not ship SDK classification logic that relies on “best effort” server behavior unless the fallback is safe.

Monica: set up a 30 min exec check-in next week with you + Ibrahim (as TL) so we can review standards/ADR drafts and the deprecation plan.

— Nikhil
Nikhil Sharma
VP Engineering | Redwood Inference


From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>
To: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 15, 2026 at 11:17 AM PST
Subject: Re: SDK parity kickoff + exec visibility (8-week plan)

Got it.

On defaults vs flags:
- Proposal: ship “v1 standard” as the default in the next minor for each SDK *only where it is additive/non-breaking* (error typing improvements, request_id surfacing, Retry-After parsing support, env var alignment w/ deprecation warnings).
- For behavior likely to break expectations (notably timeout defaults + some retry edge cases), we ship behind an explicit opt-in config flag for one release, accompanied by:
  - deprecation warning if customers use legacy names/options
  - migration guide w/ “recommended production config” for each language
- Then we flip defaults in the subsequent minor (target ~4–6 weeks after first release), assuming dashboards are green and beta feedback is solid.

Rollback triggers (draft):
- Retry rate: +X% vs baseline per route/model, sustained 15 min
- 429 amplification: increase in 429s *correlated* with client retry_count distribution shift
- Timeout rate: increase in client-side timeouts (by SDK) beyond threshold
- Streaming disconnect reasons: spike in mid-stream disconnects in enterprise regions / proxies

Where metrics live:
- We’re drafting a small “SDK retry/timeout/stream” dashboard spec (dimensions: sdk_language, sdk_version, status_code, retry_count, timeout_ms bucket, streaming_disconnect_reason) — we can instrument via SDK hooks (where allowed) and at minimum standardized logging fields.
- Support/Oncall will ask for request_id + sdk_version consistently (also updating macros).

Ethan: re Retry-After strictness — the standard will be:
- If Retry-After present and parseable: honor it (bounded by max_delay)
- If missing: use exponential backoff with jitter
- Always cap attempts and total elapsed time; never infinite retry

I’ll schedule the 30 min check-in for Tue/Wed next week with Ibrahim included.

— Monica


From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 15, 2026 at 4:41 PM PST
Subject: Re: SDK parity kickoff + exec visibility (8-week plan)

Update on platform deps:
- Retry-After on 429: we can land this early next week and roll out behind the gateway config flag first (safe).
- request_id propagation: we already have a patch drafted; likely lands same week.
- error code enum cleanup: might take slightly longer because a couple services emit legacy codes; but we can at least guarantee gateway normalization for the SDK consumption path.

So: you can assume “consistent Retry-After + request_id” by end of Week 2, and “normalized error code field” by Week 3.

Please send the final list of retryable codes once the ADR is close; I’d like to ensure the gateway codes align with what the clients will treat as retryable.

— Ethan


From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>
To: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 16, 2026 at 2:35 PM PST
Subject: Re: SDK parity kickoff + exec visibility (8-week plan)

Perfect — Week 2/3 platform timing works.

Calendar hold coming for a 30 min exec check-in:
- Wed Jan 21, 10:00–10:30a PT
Attendees: Monica, Ibrahim (TL), Ethan (platform), Nikhil
Agenda:
1) Parity matrix highlights (top mismatches driving support)
2) Draft retry policy + timeout model (what’s default vs flagged)
3) Telemetry/logging fields + rollout dashboards + rollback triggers
4) Migration + customer comms plan (docs + release notes)

Also attaching the initial timeline/risk sheet we’ll maintain weekly.

— Monica
Monica Patel
Director, SDK Engineering | Redwood Inference

From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 15, 2026 at 4:41 PM PST
Subject: Re: SDK parity kickoff + exec visibility (8-week plan)

Update on platform deps:
- Retry-After on 429: we can land this early next week and roll out behind the gateway config flag first (safe).
- request_id propagation: we already have a patch drafted; likely lands same week.
- error code enum cleanup: might take slightly longer because a couple services emit legacy codes; but we can at least guarantee gateway normalization for the SDK consumption path.

So: you can assume “consistent Retry-After + request_id” by end of Week 2, and “normalized error code field” by Week 3.

Please send the final list of retryable codes once the ADR is close; I’d like to ensure the gateway codes align with what the clients will treat as retryable.

— Ethan

source.subject: Re: Threat model review: Offline bundle signing (Private on-prem)
source.body:
From: Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwoodinference.com>, Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sahana Iyer <sahana_iyer@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>, Olga Petrov <olga_petrov@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Nov 18, 2025 at 9:12 AM PST
Subject: Threat model review: Offline bundle signing (Private on-prem)

Marcus/Aisha,

We’ve got the threat model draft for Offline Bundle Signing (Redwood Private on-prem) ready for security review.

Ask:
- Please review and call out required mitigations and explicit sign-off criteria for Phase 1 (pilot-ready).
- We’re aiming to lock scope for the release train by Fri (11/21) and want to make sure we’re not missing any “must-have” controls.

Key design assumptions (current):
- Offline bundle includes a canonical manifest + detached signature
- Installer verifies signature + hashes during preflight and fails closed (with a break-glass override gated by policy)
- Trust roots shipped in installer with support for key rollover (multiple trusted pubkeys)

Attachments:
- [Attachment: threat-model-offline-bundle-signing_v1.1.pdf]
- [Attachment: offline-bundle-signing-mitigation-tracker.xlsx]

Thanks,
Connor O’Brien
Release Engineering Lead
Redwood Inference


---

From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwoodinference.com>
To: Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sahana Iyer <sahana_iyer@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>, Olga Petrov <olga_petrov@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Nov 18, 2025 at 11:03 AM PST
Subject: Re: Threat model review: Offline bundle signing (Private on-prem)

Reviewed v1.1. Overall direction is solid. Main feedback is to tighten (a) downgrade/replay semantics, (b) build/signing key custody evidence, and (c) failure mode clarity so we consistently fail closed.

Top findings / required mitigations (Phase 1 “must”)

1) Tampering (bundle content / manifest)
- Must: Installer verifies signature over a canonicalized manifest AND verifies the per-artifact hashes listed in that manifest.
- Must: Canonicalization rules need to be frozen and tested (ordering, encoding, whitespace). Otherwise we’ll get signature mismatch across build envs.
- Must: Verification fails closed on any mismatch (sig invalid, hash mismatch, missing artifact referenced by manifest).

2) Replay + downgrade attacks
- Must: Signed manifest MUST include at least:
  - product_line (redwood-private), bundle_version, build_id, and a monotonic “release_version” (or equivalent)
  - issued_at (or signed build time) and signing_key_id
- Must: Installer maintains a local state record of last installed release_version/build_id and blocks downgrade by default.
- Should: Clarify acceptable rollback process (e.g., explicit “allow-rollback” flag + local audit marker). Today the doc implies rollback may be supported; security-wise it’s fine only with explicit operator intent + strong logging.

3) Signing key compromise / misuse
- Must: Signing must happen in a protected environment with strong separation of duties.
  - At minimum: no long-lived signing private key material in CI runner filesystem.
  - Prefer HSM-backed key or KMS/HSM with attested signing (I know we have constraints; still need a clear policy statement + controls).
- Must: Two-person rule for any manual signing / emergency signing.
- Must: Publish (internally) a revocation/rotation process and how installers learn new trust roots in air-gapped environments.

4) CI/build pipeline integrity
- Must: Reproducibility notes are good, but we need concrete gates:
  - manifest generation is deterministic and in the same pipeline step as signing (or at least in the same provenance chain)
  - provenance/audit log: who triggered the release, which git sha, which build system identity, which key id
- Should: Consider signing a digest of the bundle (or tarball) in addition to manifest; if not, clearly justify why manifest+hashes is sufficient.

5) Certificate validity / time skew (air-gapped)
- Must: Avoid any design that becomes “bricked” by lack of accurate time.
  - If using cert chains with notBefore/notAfter, specify time-source expectations.
  - If using raw public keys, reduce time dependencies.
- Must: Installer error messaging should specifically identify time validity failures vs unknown key vs tamper.

6) Break-glass path
- Must: Break-glass must be explicitly gated (flag + documented approval), and it must emit a loud local audit event.
- Must: Break-glass cannot silently proceed; operator must acknowledge risk.

Sign-off criteria (Security Eng) for Phase 1 / pilot-ready
- ADR is merged and explicitly states: algorithm, canonicalization, signature envelope, trust roots, and downgrade policy.
- Installer:
  - preflight verifies signature + all hashes
  - fails closed on all errors
  - emits local audit events for verify success/failure (include reason codes)
  - implements downgrade prevention with explicit override for rollback
  - has an inspect/verify CLI output that Support/SE can use
- Release pipeline:
  - protected signing step (documented key custody)
  - auditable trail (approvals + key id + build id)
  - tests include tamper scenarios (modified manifest, swapped artifact, truncated bundle)
- Docs/runbooks:
  - key rotation/trust store update instructions for air-gapped customers
  - break-glass policy page (who can approve, what evidence required)

If the team agrees, I can mark “conditional sign-off” once I see the updated ADR language + a brief key custody section in the CI doc.

Marcus Lin
Security Engineering Lead
Redwood Inference


---

From: Sahana Iyer <sahana_iyer@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>, Olga Petrov <olga_petrov@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Nov 18, 2025 at 12:06 PM PST
Subject: Re: Threat model review: Offline bundle signing (Private on-prem)

- We’ll add a gate: signing step must be invoked only for tagged releases from protected branches.

On determinism:
- Jordan is already drafting canonical JSON rules; we’ll add a unit test that round-trips manifest generation and ensures stable output across platforms.

From: Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwoodinference.com>, Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sahana Iyer <sahana_iyer@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>, Olga Petrov <olga_petrov@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Nov 18, 2025 at 9:12 AM PST
Subject: Threat model review: Offline bundle signing (Private on-prem)

Marcus/Aisha,

We’ve got the threat model draft for Offline Bundle Signing (Redwood Private on-prem) ready for security review.

Ask:
- Please review and call out required mitigations and explicit sign-off criteria for Phase 1 (pilot-ready).
- We’re aiming to lock scope for the release train by Fri (11/21) and want to make sure we’re not missing any “must-have” controls.

Key design assumptions (current):
- Offline bundle includes a canonical manifest + detached signature
- Installer verifies signature + hashes during preflight and fails closed (with a break-glass override gated by policy)
- Trust roots shipped in installer with support for key rollover (multiple trusted pubkeys)

Attachments:
- [Attachment: threat-model-offline-bundle-signing_v1.1.pdf]
- [Attachment: offline-bundle-signing-mitigation-tracker.xlsx]

Thanks,
Connor O’Brien
Release Engineering Lead
Redwood Inference


---

From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwoodinference.com>
To: Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sahana Iyer <sahana_iyer@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>, Olga Petrov <olga_petrov@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Nov 18, 2025 at 11:03 AM PST
Subject: Re: Threat model review: Offline bundle signing (Private on-prem)

Reviewed v1.1. Overall direction is solid. Main feedback is to tighten (a) downgrade/replay semantics, (b) build/signing key custody evidence, and (c) failure mode clarity so we consistently fail closed.

Top findings / required mitigations (Phase 1 “must”)

1) Tampering (bundle content / manifest)
- Must: Installer verifies signature over a canonicalized manifest AND verifies the per-artifact hashes listed in that manifest.
- Must: Canonicalization rules need to be frozen and tested (ordering, encoding, whitespace). Otherwise we’ll get signature mismatch across build envs.
- Must: Verification fails closed on any mismatch (sig invalid, hash mismatch, missing artifact referenced by manifest).

2) Replay + downgrade attacks
- Must: Signed manifest MUST include at least:
  - product_line (redwood-private), bundle_version, build_id, and a monotonic “release_version” (or equivalent)
  - issued_at (or signed build time) and signing_key_id
- Must: Installer maintains a local state record of last installed release_version/build_id and blocks downgrade by default.
- Should: Clarify acceptable rollback process (e.g., explicit “allow-rollback” flag + local audit marker). Today the doc implies rollback may be supported; security-wise it’s fine only with explicit operator intent + strong logging.

3) Signing key compromise / misuse
- Must: Signing must happen in a protected environment with strong separation of duties.
  - At minimum: no long-lived signing private key material in CI runner filesystem.
  - Prefer HSM-backed key or KMS/HSM with attested signing (I know we have constraints; still need a clear policy statement + controls).
- Must: Two-person rule for any manual signing / emergency signing.
- Must: Publish (internally) a revocation/rotation process and how installers learn new trust roots in air-gapped environments.

4) CI/build pipeline integrity
- Must: Reproducibility notes are good, but we need concrete gates:
  - manifest generation is deterministic and in the same pipeline step as signing (or at least in the same provenance chain)
  - provenance/audit log: who triggered the release, which git sha, which build system identity, which key id
- Should: Consider signing a digest of the bundle (or tarball) in addition to manifest; if not, clearly justify why manifest+hashes is sufficient.

5) Certificate validity / time skew (air-gapped)
- Must: Avoid any design that becomes “bricked” by lack of accurate time.
  - If using cert chains with notBefore/notAfter, specify time-source expectations.
  - If using raw public keys, reduce time dependencies.
- Must: Installer error messaging should specifically identify time validity failures vs unknown key vs tamper.

6) Break-glass path
- Must: Break-glass must be explicitly gated (flag + documented approval), and it must emit a loud local audit event.
- Must: Break-glass cannot silently proceed; operator must acknowledge risk.

Sign-off criteria (Security Eng) for Phase 1 / pilot-ready
- ADR is merged and explicitly states: algorithm, canonicalization, signature envelope, trust roots, and downgrade policy.
- Installer:
  - preflight verifies signature + all hashes
  - fails closed on all errors
  - emits local audit events for verify success/failure (include reason codes)
  - implements downgrade prevention with explicit override for rollback
  - has an inspect/verify CLI output that Support/SE can use
- Release pipeline:
  - protected signing step (documented key custody)
  - auditable trail (approvals + key id + build id)
  - tests include tamper scenarios (modified manifest, swapped artifact, truncated bundle)
- Docs/runbooks:
  - key rotation/trust store update instructions for air-gapped customers
  - break-glass policy page (who can approve, what evidence required)

If the team agrees, I can mark “conditional sign-off” once I see the updated ADR language + a brief key custody section in the CI doc.

Marcus Lin
Security Engineering Lead
Redwood Inference

source.subject: Go/No-Go: us-east canary for Hosted Overload Protection (circuit breakers + load shedding) — 11/18
source.body:
From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Aisha Bello <aisha.bello@redwoodinference.com>, Christine Wong <christine.wong@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>, Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Nov 18, 2025 at 8:58 AM PST
Subject: Go/No-Go: us-east canary for Hosted Overload Protection (circuit breakers + load shedding) — 11/18

Team — proposing we do the us-east canary enablement today (target window 12:30–2:00pm PT) for Hosted Overload Protection.

Scope for canary (Phase 2):
- Region: us-east
- Tenants: 5% of self-serve hosted tenants, exclude Dedicated + enterprise hosted (explicit allowlist)
- Routes: /v1/chat/completions and /v1/completions initially
- Features: gateway admission control + overload error taxonomy (429/503) + Retry-After
- Fallback routing: enabled only when tenant has explicit fallback configured (no global fallback)

Go/No-Go checklist status (high level):
- [GREEN] Error taxonomy stable + SDK guidance drafted (ADR-014 aligned)
- [GREEN] Breaker policy defaults staged (no per-tenant overrides unless allowlisted)
- [GREEN] Dashboards + alerts deployed in prod (initial tuning done)
- [YELLOW] Retry-After header coverage: one known gap on legacy completion route behind old gateway path; fix queued for tonight if we proceed

Monitoring gates (must hold for 60 min post-enable):
1) p99 latency: no >10% regression vs 7-day baseline for canary cohort (overall us-east)
2) 5xx: no sustained >0.2% increase (excluding 503 overload)
3) Overload shed rate: <1% sustained for canary cohort (spikes ok if correlated to burst tests)
4) Breaker open rate: <0.5% sustained on any single model/route combo
5) Error budget burn: no "fast burn" alert on Hosted API SLO

Rollback criteria (any one triggers rollback within 10 minutes):
- p99 latency +20% for >10 min and correlated with breaker activity
- 503 overload without Retry-After on >5% of overload responses for canary cohort
- Increase in client retry storms (gateway RPS +30% without matching unique request IDs)
- Customer escalation / high-sev support ticket attributable to canary behavior

Rollback mechanics:
- Flip feature flag: gateway.admission_control.enabled=false (us-east)
- Flip routing flag: routing.overload_fallback.enabled=false (us-east)
- Revert policy snapshot to "baseline-2025-11-10" in policy store

Please reply with GO/NO-GO by 11:30am PT with any blockers.

Links:
- Canary dashboard: https://grafana.redwoodinference.com/d/hosted-overload-canary/us-east-canary
- Hosted API SLO: https://grafana.redwoodinference.com/d/hosted-api-slos/hosted-api-slo
- Checklist doc: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1RWD-ga-checklist-overload-protection

— Sean

To: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Aisha Bello <aisha.bello@redwoodinference.com>, Christine Wong <christine.wong@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>, Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Nov 18, 2025 at 10:19 AM PST
Subject: Re: Go/No-Go: us-east canary for Hosted Overload Protection (circuit breakers + load shedding) — 11/18

Updated plan (per Ethan + Mina + Rafael):

Today’s canary scope (11/18):
- Region: us-east
- Tenants: 5% self-serve hosted (explicit allowlist), excluding Dedicated + enterprise hosted
- Routes: /v1/chat/completions ONLY
- Behavior: gateway admission control + overload error taxonomy (429/503) + Retry-After
- Routing fallback: only for explicitly configured tenants; bounded to 1 hop

Monitoring gates unchanged, but Retry-After missing gate should be effectively moot since chat route is on new middleware.

Rollout timeline:
- 12:30pm PT enable
- 12:40pm PT validate metrics/labels
- 1:30pm PT go/no-go to keep enabled overnight

Connor — please capture as release note: "us-east chat-only canary, 5% self-serve allowlist".

Thanks all.

— Sean


From: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Aisha Bello <aisha.bello@redwoodinference.com>, Christine Wong <christine.wong@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>, Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Nov 18, 2025 at 3:41 PM PST
Subject: Re: Go/No-Go: us-east canary for Hosted Overload Protection (circuit breakers + load shedding) — 11/18

Captured in release notes + #eng-oncall calendar.

I’ll post a short heads-up in #support as well: new overload 503s may appear for a small subset of self-serve in us-east, with Retry-After + correlation ID.

Ping me when you flip the flag so I can timestamp it.

— Connor
Release & Canary Coordination | Redwood Inference

source.subject: Demo tenant access request + scoped API keys (rotation + sharing rules)
source.body:
From: Markus Klein <markus_klein@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie_nguyen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Nov 8, 2025 at 9:12 AM
Subject: Demo tenant access request + scoped API keys (rotation + sharing rules)

Hey Ethan,

We’re ramping the demo tenant usage next week (pilot AEs are scheduled for a few runs). Can you help us lock down the access + key flow so we’re not passing around "god keys"?

What we need:
- RBAC: AE/SE access to the Demo org in Console (view dashboards, view routes, run evals) but *no* org-level admin.
- API keys: one per demo project, scoped to the demo scenarios only (chat completions + embeddings + rerank, no billing/admin endpoints).
- Rotation: a simple cadence + process (weekly seems fine?) and a way to revoke quickly if something leaks.

Also, can we confirm the current best practice on sharing? I want this explicitly written so it’s consistent:
- Not in Slack
- Not in customer-facing docs
- Prefer 1Password vault link / time-limited secret

If you have an internal doc you want us to point field folks to, send it over and we’ll include in the SE runbook.

Thanks,
Markus

—
Markus Klein
Sales Engineering (Demo Technical Owner)
Redwood Inference



From: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwoodinference.com>
To: Markus Klein <markus_klein@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie_nguyen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Nov 8, 2025 at 9:27 AM
Subject: Re: Demo tenant access request + scoped API keys (rotation + sharing rules)

+1 — I have Northstar follow-up Monday and need a key that won’t accidentally let me nuke something.

Practical asks from the field:
- I need *two* keys if we do parallel demos (SE + me running the scripted app in separate terminals), or at least keys per “namespace/project”.
- Ideally a “read-only” Console role for exec demos (I click around, but no config changes).

Also: can we put an expiration on keys? Even 24h would reduce anxiety.

Avery



From: Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Markus Klein <markus_klein@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie_nguyen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Nov 8, 2025 at 10:03 AM
Subject: Re: Demo tenant access request + scoped API keys (rotation + sharing rules)

Yep — we can do this cleanly. Proposed model:

1) RBAC roles (Demo org)
- "Demo Viewer": Console read-only (dashboards, traces, usage, model catalog), cannot edit routes/projects.
- "Demo Operator": can run evals + trigger synthetic traffic job + view rollout status; cannot change org settings, cannot create new users, cannot change billing.

I’ll map AEs to Demo Viewer by default, SEs to Demo Operator.

2) API keys
We’ll stop issuing “org admin” keys.
- Keys will be created at the *project* level: demo-core-<date> / demo-econ-<date> / demo-reliability-<date>
- Scopes:
  - inference:chat.completions (incl streaming)
  - inference:embeddings
  - inference:rerank
  - evals:run (optional — SE only)
  - routes:read (so the demo app can display pinned model metadata)
  - NO: org:admin / billing:* / keys:* / users:* / audit:* write

3) Expiration + rotation
- We can’t do true “auto-expire” on API keys today without breaking some tooling (working on it), but we *can* do:
  - Named keys per demo date
  - Enforced rotation every Monday (we’ll revoke the prior week’s keys)
  - Emergency revoke path (I can do it, or we can grant Sean as backup)

4) Sharing rules (hard requirements)
- Do not paste keys in Slack channels (including #sales)
- Do not screenshot keys
- Use 1Password item sharing (link) or send via Gmail *only* to internal addresses, then rotate after the demo block

If you want, I’ll draft a short “Demo tenant key handling policy” paragraph for the runbook.

Also: for parallel demos, I recommend separate projects/keys per rep (“demo-avery”, “demo-priyom”, etc.) so dashboards don’t get weird and rate limits are isolated.

Ethan



From: Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwoodinference.com>, Markus Klein <markus_klein@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwoodinference.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie_nguyen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Nov 8, 2025 at 10:26 AM
Subject: Re: Demo tenant access request + scoped API keys (rotation + sharing rules)

Adding an SRE note: please don’t give anyone a key with quota-edit / rate-limit-edit scopes.

We’ve already had one “429 during live demo” incident where folks tried to “fix it live” and made it worse. We should force the escalation path:
- If demo is throttling: run the healthcheck endpoint + ping on-call alias (don’t change quotas)
- If dashboards are empty: trigger synthetic traffic job (SE role only)

Markus — can you make sure the SE runbook links are in the talk track doc too? In the moment, people go searching in Slack.

(Also: emergency revoke should be 2 people minimum. +1 on adding me as backup revoker.)

Sean



From: Markus Klein <markus_klein@redwoodinference.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie_nguyen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Nov 8, 2025 at 12:41 PM
Subject: Re: Demo tenant access request + scoped API keys (rotation + sharing rules)

Thanks all — this is exactly what we need.

Avery: we’ll set you up with your own project + key ("demo-avery") so you can run the scripted app + keep dashboards consistent. For Monday, assume we’ll rotate keys Sunday night.

Ethan: please proceed with:
- Demo Viewer for AEs by default
- Demo Operator for SEs
- Project-per-rep approach (at least for the pilot)

Sean: agreed on the quota/edit restriction. We’ll add a bold “Do not live-edit quotas” note to the runbook + the pre-demo checklist.

Action items:
1) Ethan: create roles + assign Avery (Viewer) + Markus (Operator)
2) Ethan/Sean: confirm emergency revoke owner backup
3) Markus: update demo runbook + add a 1-paragraph “key handling” section

We’ll treat “no Slack keys” as non-negotiable.

Markus



From: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie_nguyen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Markus Klein <markus_klein@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sun, Nov 9, 2025 at 8:37 AM
Subject: Re: Demo tenant access request + scoped API keys (rotation + sharing rules)

One more thing to make this scalable:

Can we standardize the request path so reps aren’t DM’ing for access? I’d like:
- A single “Demo Tenant Access” intake (even if it’s just an email alias for now) with expected turnaround
- A rule that *recordings/screenshares must not include the API key creation screen*

I’m fine with weekly rotation as long as it’s predictable and doesn’t break Monday morning calls.

Ethan — please publish the do/don’t bullets somewhere linkable (Confluence is fine). Markus — once it exists, include it in the enablement materials.

Stephanie

—
Stephanie Nguyen
VP Sales, North America
Redwood Inference

source.subject: Q4 upside clean-up: lock expansion commits & CRM hygiene
messages:
From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Sarah Lopez <s.lopez@acmemed.com>
Date: Sat, 1 Nov 2025 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Q4 upside clean-up: lock expansion commits & CRM hygiene

Team — quick one as we head into commit reviews next week. We have a handful of expansion opportunities in Q4 tied to CS-driven adoption plays (Acme Medical included). Finance asked that we lock down committed ARR and ensure stage dates in HubSpot are accurate by EOD next Thursday so the commit meeting has clean numbers.

As the owner on several of these, I need help from CS to confirm customer intent and exact committed ARR:
- Acme Medical (Sarah/Tom) — expansion for user seats + premium support; current CRM amount: $240k upside (stage: Proposal).
- Two other customers are lower probability and listed in the tracker.

Actions: can CS confirm a committed number (or a firm no) for Acme by Tue 11/4? If yes, please update the deal record and ping Finance. Ethan — can you flag anything you need from my side for the commit call? I've attached the latest tracker and a small CSV with deal line items.

Attachments: Q4_upside_tracker.xlsx (sheet: Acme details), Acme_commit_matrix.csv

Thanks — trying to avoid noisy ask at close.

—Neha Kapoor
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
neha.kapoor@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0123

From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Sarah Lopez <s.lopez@acmemed.com>, Tom Guerrero <t.guerrero@acmemed.com>
Date: Mon, 3 Nov 2025 10:08:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Q4 upside clean-up: lock expansion commits & CRM hygiene

Neha — thanks for the heads up. I'll take point on Acme. source.subject: Fwd: Support escalation: need model/version change history (interim export until Console UI ships)
source.body:
From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Miguel Santos <miguel.santos@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Oct 10, 2025 at 9:12 AM
Subject: Support escalation: need model/version change history (interim export until Console UI ships)

Hey Chloe,

Escalating a recurring support gap that’s starting to create churn risk with enterprise/Dedicated customers.

Summary:
- We have a live ticket where a customer is reporting output drift and is asking for a definitive answer to: “What model/version did we run yesterday vs today, and what changed?”
- Today, Support can’t reliably self-serve the model change history without asking Eng to manually look across registry/config/releases.
- Even if the change was “non-breaking,” the absence of a customer-facing change log is being interpreted as a silent change.

Most recent example:
- SUP-28429 (Lattice Health Systems) — Dedicated deployment. Reported output regression starting ~Oct 9 18:00 PT. They are pinned to a model alias in their integration, not a hard version.
- SUP-28411 (different enterprise) — asked why a model “seemed faster but different” after a promotion; we couldn’t provide release notes quickly.

What we need (interim, internal-only):
1) A way for Support/TAMs to pull “model/version change history” for a given account + deployment mode (Hosted/Dedicated/Private) + time range.
2) Ideally exportable to CSV so we can attach to the ticket + share a sanitized version with customer.
3) Must not leak internal-only notes (esp. the root-cause / rollout notes that are fine for admins but not customers).

We know the proper Console UI is coming (ENG-9824), but we need a stopgap for the next few weeks. Even an internal endpoint or script + runbook would help.

Can you advise who owns the fastest path for an internal export? Happy to help test with real cases.

Thanks,
Dev

—
Dev Patel
Support Escalations
Redwood Inference

Attachment: SUP-28429-summary.txt



From: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Miguel Santos <miguel.santos@redwoodinference.com>, Jada Williams <jada.williams@redwoodinference.com>, Michael Nguyen <michael.nguyen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Oct 10, 2025 at 10:03 AM
Subject: Re: Support escalation: need model/version change history (interim export until Console UI ships)

Dev — thanks for sending with concrete tickets.

Agree this is a gap (and aligns with what we’re building in Console), but we should be able to give you an interim internal tool/export.

A couple quick clarifying questions so we don’t ship the wrong thing:
- For Dedicated, do you need this by “account id” (customer org) and “deployment id/cluster”, or is org-level enough?
- When you say “model alias,” are they pinned to something like redwood://models/<family>:latest, or are they using our routing policy layer (i.e., eligible promotion changes under the hood)?
- Do you need per-request history (what model served each request), or the list of promotion events (what changed and when)?

Looping in Jada + Michael because they’re closest to the eventing/changelog work. My intuition: the fastest path is to query the model promotion/audit events we’re emitting and expose a locked-down admin endpoint (Support RBAC) + CSV export.

We should also be careful here given the recent issue where internal notes briefly showed up in rollout UI (PR-18463). Any interim solution needs redaction baked in.

Dev/Miguel — can you reply with:
1) the customer org slug / internal account id for Lattice
2) the exact time window where they saw drift
3) what you want the customer-facing narrative to include (just “version changed,” or high-level perf/quality category)

Chloe

—
Chloe Martin
Engineering Manager, Console Backend
Redwood Inference



From: Miguel Santos <miguel.santos@redwoodinference.com>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Jada Williams <jada.williams@redwoodinference.com>, Michael Nguyen <michael.nguyen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Oct 10, 2025 at 10:22 AM
Subject: Re: Support escalation: need model/version change history (interim export until Console UI ships)

Adding CS context (I’m the CS lead on Lattice):

- They’re Dedicated, single region (us-west-2), cluster “lat-prod-01” (from our onboarding doc).
- They use the alias “rw.chat/atlas-13b” (their code references the alias; they did not pin a specific semantic version).
- They’re asking for:
  a) “Did you change the model?” (yes/no)
  b) “When did you change it?” (timestamp)
  c) “What changed / how risky?” (high-level, not internal details)
  d) “How do we roll back?” (instructions or we do it for them)

Time window:
- First report: Oct 9 ~6:10pm PT
- Most acute: Oct 9 6:30pm–8:00pm PT

What we need quickly is the promotion/change event view. Per-request history would be nice but not required for this ticket.

If we can get a CSV export with: timestamp, alias, old_version, new_version, deployment_id/cluster, and a public-safe summary category, we can respond confidently.

Miguel

—
Miguel Santos
Enterprise Customer Success
Redwood Inference



From: Jada Williams <jada.williams@redwoodinference.com>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Miguel Santos <miguel.santos@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Michael Nguyen <michael.nguyen@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Oct 10, 2025 at 11:08 AM
Subject: Re: Support escalation: need model/version change history (interim export until Console UI ships)

+1 that “promotion events only” gets us 80% of the value quickly.

What we have today:
- Control plane is emitting structured events for model metadata changes + version promotions (this is the pipeline we’re using for the changelog generator work).
- Internally, we can query the event store, but there isn’t a Support-friendly surface.

Fastest interim option (1–2 days):
- Michael and I can provide a one-off script + runbook that:
  - takes org/account id + deployment id + time range
  - queries promotion events (alias -> concrete version) and deprecation/rollback events
  - applies the public/internal redaction rules (i.e., only include fields flagged public)
  - outputs CSV

Near-term productized option (1–2 weeks, but behind admin RBAC):
- Add an internal-only endpoint in Console backend (or control plane admin) like:
  GET /admin/model-change-events?account_id=...&deployment_id=...&since=...&until=...
- Response includes a “public_summary” field that can be safely copied into a customer email (aligned to release note taxonomy categories).

Constraints:
- For Dedicated, we need to be careful about “deployment_id” vs “cluster name” mapping, but we can start with deployment_id and add lookup.
- We should explicitly exclude any internal_only notes and anything that mentions capacity/incident context.

Dev/Miguel: if you send me the internal account_id (or org slug) for Lattice, I can have Michael run the event query for the Oct 9 window today.

Jada

—
Jada Williams
Staff Engineer, Console & Rollouts
Redwood Inference



From: Michael Nguyen <michael.nguyen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Jada Williams <jada.williams@redwoodinference.com>, Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Miguel Santos <miguel.santos@redwoodinference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Oct 10, 2025 at 1:34 PM
Subject: Re: Support escalation: need model/version change history (interim export until Console UI ships)

I can take the “one-off export now” plus draft the runbook.

Notes on data sources:
- Promotion events: emitted from control plane on alias promotion + rollback (covers what Support needs for “did you change it / when”).
- Audit events: emitted for metadata changes (covers “what changed,” but we need to keep this high-level / public-safe).

I’ll produce a CSV with these columns:
- event_time
- account_id
- deployment_id
- model_alias
- from_version
- to_version
- change_type (PROMOTE | ROLLBACK | DEPRECATE)
- customer_visible_category (perf | quality | bugfix | deprecation | breaking)

Michael

source.subject: Acme procurement: marketplace vs direct — path & timeline
messages:
From: Lisa Grant <lisa.grant@acme-corp.com>
To: Hannah Schmitt <hannah_schmitt@redwood.ai>
Cc: procurement-team@acme-corp.com
Date: 2025-10-01T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Acme procurement question: marketplace vs direct

Hi Hannah,

Thanks for the discussion yesterday. Procurement is trying to decide whether to buy Redwood via AWS Marketplace (we're AWS-first) or sign a direct contract. Can you help summarize the upside/downside from Redwood's perspective for:

- Procurement timeline (PO / SOW / signature cadence)
- Invoicing and billing (consolidated billing, net terms)
- Contract flexibility (custom SLAs, indemnities, data residency language)
- Co-sell or cloud credits implications (is there a revenue share, and does it affect pricing to us?)

We have a fairly short timeline for the POC (targeting Nov 1 start). If Marketplace would let us move faster, that matters.

Would appreciate a short write-up and any docs you can share. cc'd our procurement inbox.

Thanks,
Lisa Grant
Senior Procurement Manager
Acme Corporation

---
From: Hannah Schmitt <hannah_schmitt@redwood.ai>
To: Lisa Grant <lisa.grant@acme-corp.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.ai>
Date: 2025-10-02T10:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Acme procurement question: marketplace vs direct

Lisa — thanks for the clear ask. Attached two short docs: a one-pager SLA/ops summary for Marketplace vs Direct, and a spreadsheet that explains how co-sell credits and revenue share affect pricing tiers. High level summary below; happy to hop on a quick call to walk through.

Marketplace (AWS/Azure/GCP) — pros:
- Fast procurement: procurement can often place an order and get product access in 3–10 business days depending on internal approval flows.
- Consolidated billing: charges appear on the customer cloud invoice; good for customers that prefer a single vendor bill.
- Standardized T&Cs: easier for procurement to accept because fewer redlines (some customers treat marketplace purchases as "off-the-shelf").
- Co-sell benefits: for AWS/Azure we participate in partner programs that can surface Redwood to internal account teams and sometimes provide co-sell incentives.

Marketplace — cons / caveats:
- Limited custom contract language: most marketplaces disallow deep redlines to indemnity/data residency/SLAs. If you need bespoke terms, direct contracting wins.
- Pricing and revenue share: cloud marketplaces typically retain a 3–20% take rate (varies by program and cloud), which impacts list price or partner margins; we can work on net pricing for you but the commercial math differs versus direct.
- SLA and support entitlements: enterprise SLAs and bespoke escalation paths are easier to commit to in a direct agreement.

Direct contract — pros:
- Full commercial flexibility: custom SLAs, security addenda, and data residency commitments can be written into the SOW.
- Pricing: no marketplace take; discounts and committed reserved capacity (Dedicated) can be structured directly.
- Enterprise governance: better for customers requiring audit logs, longer retention, custom KMS/HSM arrangements.

Direct contract — cons:
- Longer timeline: legal + procurement + security reviews typically add 6–12 weeks (could be faster with standardized Redwood templates and pre-authorized terms).
- More negotiation overhead and potential for multiple redline rounds.

Recommendation (given your Nov 1 POC start target):
- If Acme's priority is speed-to-PoC and you do not require custom contract clauses or additional indemnities, go Marketplace for the POC (we can issue a marketplace listing with trial credits and a short SOW).
- If you require contractual commitments (data residency, SOC 2 addendum, specific SLA targets) for the POC, begin a parallel direct contracting track and we can run a phased MSA + SOW with a short-term POC addendum.

Next steps: I attached the two docs — Redwood_Marketplace_SLA_Summary.pdf and CoSell_Program_Terms.xlsx. Jordan, can you add a note on reserved capacity pricing and how that works if Acme later converts to Dedicated?

Happy to schedule a 30-min procurement walk-through this week.

Best,
Hannah Schmitt
Head of GTM Ops (Redwood Inference)
hannah_schmitt@redwood.ai
+1 415-555-0198

Attachments: Redwood_Marketplace_SLA_Summary.pdf (1 page), CoSell_Program_Terms.xlsx (spreadsheet)

---
From: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.ai>
To: Lisa Grant <lisa.grant@acme-corp.com>, Hannah Schmitt <hannah_schmitt@redwood.ai>
Cc: procurement-team@acme-corp.com
Date: 2025-10-03T14:18:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Acme procurement question: marketplace vs direct

Quick add from me on pricing and Dedicated conversion:

- Reserved / Dedicated pricing: if Acme anticipates predictable steady traffic, we can model a Dedicated commit that lowers unit costs materially. That pricing is only available via direct contracting (not marketplace).
- Conversion path: we support a staged conversion — POC on Marketplace, then convert with a simple migration SOW to Dedicated. We'll make sure KV cache and model version pinning are preserved.
- Discounts: we can offer blended discounts when you commit term + capacity; Jordan can run a model.

If you want numbers, share an expected token/hour profile and we’ll produce a 3-tier estimate (POC, pilot, production).

-Jordan
Sr. Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference

---
From: Lisa Grant <lisa.grant@acme-corp.com>
To: Hannah Schmitt <hannah_schmitt@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.ai>
Cc: procurement-team@acme-corp.com
Date: 2025-10-06T08:46:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Acme procurement question: marketplace vs direct

Thanks Hannah and Jordan — very helpful. A couple follow-ups: 

1) Data residency: Acme requires EU+US regional isolation for customer data. If we start via AWS Marketplace in the US, can we guarantee that PII and logs won’t transit outside regions? Is this a Marketplace limitation?
